Just in: early morning! Across the board, Goldman Sachs made a big statement! U.S. stocks, gold and silver rise collectively.

Early this morning, at the beginning of the Asia-Pacific session, major U.S. stock index futures rose across the board, with Nasdaq 100 index futures soaring by more than 1%; the precious metals market also strengthened collectively. Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest report that it has captured the initial dip buying signal of "U.S.

momentum stocks" and that the momentum factor has the basis for a tactical rebound. At the same time, international oil prices continued to fall, which also eased market concerns about the risk of rising inflation. On the news, OPEC+ has agreed to increase production quotas by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

Wall Street institutions such as Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs have recently warned that the international crude oil market may be at risk of oversupply next year. In the morning of July 6, Beijing time, the three major U.S. stock index futures collectively rose.

As of 7:20, Nasdaq 100 futures rose 1.45%, S&P 500 futures rose 0.5%, and Dow futures rose 0.04%. The precious metals market also rose slightly, with spot silver rising 1.2% to US$63.11 per ounce and spot gold rising 0.37% to US$4,189.54 per ounce. U.S.

stocks were closed for holidays last Friday, and the share prices of Korean chip giants rose sharply across the board. As of the close, SK Hynix rose 10.88% and Samsung Electronics rose 8.22%. In the previous two trading days, U.S. chip stocks fell sharply, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index falling by more than 11%.

The Goldman Sachs Trading Desk pointed out that the current decline in "U.S. momentum stocks" has provided room for short-term tactical rebounds - historical experience shows that the momentum factor often records positive returns during the correction phase.

However, Goldman Sachs reminded that momentum factor positions are still highly crowded, and if the deleveraging trend continues, the maximum potential retracement may still be twice the current decline.

Overall, the market is in the early stages of clearing momentum strategy positions, and the key to subsequent interpretation lies in the game between the liquidity environment and the rhythm of emotional repair.

Data from Goldman Sachs shows that the momentum factor has fallen by 24% since its peak, the largest retracement since the first quarter of 2023, which is significantly higher than the historical average retracement of about 12%. Looking at the adjustment cycle, the historical average lasts for about 24 days, while the current one lasts only 10 days.

Goldman Sachs pointed out that since 2026, high beta momentum strategies have experienced multiple losses of more than 10% within two days, and have since recovered to varying degrees. The current market trend is highly consistent with the previous model.

The bank believes that this round of amplified decline is mainly affected by structural factors such as low liquidity and weak summer trading, and is not a fundamental change in the fundamental trend.

Judging from historical experience, the winning rate of the bargain-hunting strategy has significantly improved after the Federal Reserve’s policy turns to easing (such as during the epidemic).

Goldman Sachs specifically mentioned that the market's doubts about the return rate on its capital expenditures caused by Meta's transformation to cloud business are similar to the path of impact on market sentiment caused by similar concerns in the past.

The bank's trading department currently judges that the core narrative of AI has not yet undergone structural changes that are sufficient to drive a deeper correction. International oil prices continue to fall International oil prices continued to fall.

As of 7:20 Beijing time, WTI crude oil futures fell 0.52% to US$68.33/barrel; Brent crude oil futures fell 0.61% to US$71.68/barrel. On the news, according to Bloomberg, OPEC+ has agreed to increase production quotas by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

This quota increase of 188,000 barrels per day will bring OPEC+’s cumulative new quotas since the outbreak of the war to 940,000 barrels per day, equivalent to nearly 1% of global demand. The above-mentioned production increase plan is jointly promoted by seven major member countries, led by Saudi Arabia and Russia.

According to previous reports by Bloomberg, OPEC+ has formulated a roadmap and plans to complete the full cancellation of the two rounds of production cuts in 2023 by September through continued quota increases.

The interim peace agreement between the United States and Iran has cleared key obstacles for the Persian Gulf oil-producing countries to resume exports.

According to tanker tracking data, oil exports from Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates have basically returned to pre-war levels, and the freight channels between the two countries through the Strait of Hormuz have been successfully opened. However, data compiled by Bloomberg show that the actual output of the two countries is still far below normal.

The current recovery in exports mainly relies on the digestion of early inventory, rather than the simultaneous recovery of production capacity. It will still take time to fully release output. At the same time, there are also variables in the situation in the Strait of Hormuz.

According to CCTV News, data released by the US-led Joint Maritime Information Center on the 5th showed that in the 72 hours from July 2 to 4, a total of 70 merchant ships were escorted by the US through the Strait of Hormuz. The scale of navigation was far lower than the average daily average of 138 ships before the conflict.

This data was released by the British Maritime Trade Action Office on the same day. The announcement shows that the daily traffic volume of U.S.-coordinated escort ships continues to decline: 33 ships on July 2, 29 ships on July 3, and only 18 ships on July 4.

There are currently two navigation channels in the Strait of Hormuz, namely the southern channel near Oman and the northern channel controlled by Iran. The traffic volume of the southern channel escorted by the United States has not continued to increase, and the actual total navigation volume of the northern channel is difficult to accurately calculate.

The announcement rated the threat level in the entire Strait of Hormuz as "high." Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps continues to carry out very high-frequency radio broadcasts, drone close-in reconnaissance and tracking surveillance on transiting merchant ships, and implements regular control of the Strait waterways; ships that continue to turn on the automatic ship identification system signal may receive warnings requiring diversion to Iranian-controlled waterways.

The announcement also stated that there is still a risk of mines in the Strait waters, and relevant mine clearance survey operations are being carried out across the entire area; interference to global navigation satellite systems in the entire area is normal and will continue to pose hidden dangers to the safety of ship navigation. Just in: Overseas Research and Selection Daily 0706: Morgan Stanley: The US tariff rate may be stable at 9%-10%;.

Morgan Stanley: The U.S. tariff framework shifts to Sections 301 and 232, and the tax rate may stabilize at 9%-10% Morgan Stanley’s latest report points out that U.S. tariff policy is shifting from a temporary tool to a more legally stable framework of Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 and Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962.

With the Section 122 tariffs set to expire on July 24 and the Section 301 hearing scheduled for July 7, the U.S. tariff system will enter a critical window for reconstruction.

The bank predicts that the statutory effective tariff rate will stabilize at 9%-10% in the next few months, which is more regular than the previous high-dispersion system based on IEEPA, but industry coverage, exemptions, bilateral agreements, refunds and import structures will still affect the actual tax burden.

Morgan Stanley believes that Section 301 will become a broad, country-level basic tariff tool. The 301 investigation on forced labor issues plans to impose additional tariffs on imported goods from 60 economies: the tax rate for economies with relevant import bans or reciprocity commitments is 10%, and the rest are 12.5%.

However, USMCA compliant products, products covered by Section 232, and some textiles, information materials, etc. are exempted. Section 301 helps maintain the tariff floor and bargaining chips, but it is difficult to promote the reshoring of large-scale manufacturing on its own.

Article 232 will assume the function of targeted industrial policy, focusing on strategic areas such as steel, aluminum, copper and its derivatives, automobiles, semiconductors, pharmaceuticals, medical equipment, machinery, robots, and aerospace.

Tariffs on metal derivatives will be levied on the full customs declaration value of the product rather than just the metal content. Morgan Stanley estimates that expanding coverage will subject approximately $150 billion of goods to a 25% tariff and increase the overall tax rate by up to about 1 percentage point.

The actual burden of pharmaceutical tariffs depends on factors such as generic drugs, most-favored nation pricing, local investment commitments, and origin. In terms of refunds, the customs has opened the application channel. Review and payment usually take 60-90 days, but refunds are not completed automatically.

Morgan Stanley estimates that the upper limit of refunds will be about US$133 billion. Under the neutral scenario, about 60% of eligible tariffs, or US$84 billion, can be refunded, and in the high scenario, about US$112 billion.

The decline in tariff uncertainty will marginally support capital expenditures, but the bank emphasized that current investment is still concentrated in AI, semiconductors, data centers and power infrastructure, and evidence of the return of traditional manufacturing industries is still limited; the subsequent 232 investigation on machinery and robots will be an important node to test whether tariffs can truly expand manufacturing investment.

Goldman Sachs is bearish on the yen: it may fall to 165 in 12 months, AI investment and energy disturbances support the dollar Goldman Sachs further downgraded the outlook for the yen in its latest research report.

It is expected that the yen will remain under pressure against the US dollar under the combined influence of factors such as the maintenance of high U.S. interest rates, the moderate pace of interest rate hikes by the Bank of Japan, and Japan's fiscal expansion.

The bank raised its forecasts for the dollar against the yen in the next three months, six months and 12 months to 162, 163 and 165 respectively, significantly higher than the previous 160, 158 and 155.

Goldman Sachs believes that if the Japanese government intervenes in the currency market again, it may only be able to temporarily alleviate the depreciation of the yen. Historical experience shows that intervention can usually only temporarily interrupt the rise of the U.S. dollar against the yen. If the U.S.

economy does not experience an unexpected downturn, or the Bank of Japan does not shift to a more aggressive tightening policy, it will be difficult for the yen to form a sustained rebound. The bank pointed out that U.S.

yields are "higher and longer," recession risks are low, fiscal concerns remain, and the Bank of Japan's slow normalization of policy creates a macro backdrop for the yen's continued weakness. At the same time, Japan's fiscal stimulus plan may push up the term premium of its national debt, and changes in relative U.S.

debt have often coincided with further gains in the dollar against the yen in the past. Goldman Sachs still maintains a strong judgment on the U.S. dollar overall. The core basis is that the two major supporting factors of the U.S. AI investment boom and energy supply disruption are not expected to fade quickly.

The bank believes that the continued expansion of AI capital expenditures and recurring energy supply risks will continue to increase the attractiveness of U.S. dollar assets and put low-yielding currencies under interest rate differential pressure for a longer period of time.

Against this background, Goldman Sachs lowered its forecast for the euro, predicting that the euro against the dollar will be 1.14 in three months, fall back to 1.12 in six months, and maintain this level in 12 months.

In contrast, the bank is optimistic about some high-yielding emerging market currencies and has raised its forecast for the Indian rupee, citing improved growth, falling inflation and policy-driven capital inflows.

It is also more optimistic about the Colombian peso, due to the local central bank's hawkish stance and increased fiscal consolidation expectations. Therefore, Goldman Sachs continues to recommend using the Japanese yen as a financing currency to allocate high-yield emerging market assets, betting that low-yield currency financing transactions will continue.

UBS: The digital euro will be launched as early as 2029, and the adoption rate in Northern and Southern Europe may be significantly different.

The latest report from UBS pointed out that after the European Parliament formed a common position on the digital euro, the relevant legislation has entered the final tripartite negotiation stage with the European Commission and the Council.

If an agreement is reached in the second half of the year, regulations may be implemented in the second half of 2026, and the European Central Bank can launch a pilot in 2027 and officially launch a digital euro as early as 2029.

By then, the European Central Bank is expected to become the first major Western central bank to issue retail digital currency for residents and businesses. The digital euro will serve as the legal currency for daily payments, and merchants need to accept it in principle, and its function is similar to cash.

In order to prevent large-scale outflows of bank deposits, the individual holding limit is expected to be set at 3,000 euros without interest. Even if all households hold up to the limit, the size of the digital euro will only account for about 6% of euro area bank deposits, about 800 billion to 1 trillion euros.

The ECB hopes to use this to maintain public access to central bank currency amid a decline in cash use, reduce dependence on foreign payment institutions and stablecoins, and enhance the autonomy of the European payment system. However, UBS believes that the actual adoption rate of the digital euro may be uneven.

In a market where existing electronic payments are highly convenient, consumers lack clear motivation to switch from bank deposits to digital euros; and the Eurozone bank deposit insurance limit of 100,000 euros also weakens the safety advantage of central bank currency.

The report shows that the Eurozone may show a clear "North-South divide": Bank account coverage in Nordic countries is generally close to or exceeds 98%, and cash payment accounts for a low proportion.

The digital euro needs to compete with mature and efficient private payment systems; Southern European countries such as Italy, Greece, and Portugal have relatively low account coverage and stronger cash payment habits, and promotion requires replacing entrenched cash usage scenarios.

UBS believes that the difference in financial account penetration and cash payment preferences will become a key variable in determining the effect of the implementation of the digital euro.

Citi: The Hormuz disturbance subsides, the oil market returns to the logic of oversupply, and Brent oil may fall to $60-65 by the end of the year Citi's latest report pointed out that with the gradual recovery of shipping in the Strait of Hormuz and the high probability of the continuation of the memorandum of understanding between the United States and Iran, the geo-risk premium is fading, and the oil market is once again dominated by weak fundamentals.

Brent crude oil has fallen back to the low $70s, and Citigroup expects it to fall to $60-65 a barrel by the end of the year. Citigroup believes that disputes between the United States and Iran over strait management rights and tolls may still recur, but the two sides lack the motivation to escalate the conflict again.

Current AIS data shows that crude oil shipments in the Strait of Hormuz are close to 7 million barrels per day and product oil shipments exceed 1 million barrels per day.

Although they are still lower than the pre-conflict levels of 15 million barrels per day for crude oil and 5 million barrels per day for refined products, some ships have turned off their transponders and the actual flow rate may be higher.

Iran's potential unfreezing of approximately $12 billion in frozen assets also strengthens its willingness to keep shipping lanes open. However, supply is recovering faster than refinery demand is improving.

Chinese buyers have not yet returned significantly, and their crude oil market pull has decreased by more than 6 million barrels per day compared with the February high; global strategic reserves have shifted from absorbing 1.5 million barrels per day to a net supply of more than 2 million barrels to the commercial market.

The spot market has weakened, and the structure of Brent in recent months has turned into a futures premium, with spot Brent oil once falling to US$68.79/barrel; the discount of Angola's Dalia crude oil to spot Brent expanded to nearly US$9/barrel, a record low.

Refined oil cracking spreads are still strong, mainly due to delays in the resumption of refinery operations and disruptions in Russian refining capacity.

However, Citigroup believes that actual refining profits are weaker than the market: forward cash profits in Europe are about US$5/barrel lower than the market, and in Asia are about US$10/barrel, which inhibits refineries from increasing crude oil purchases. The operating rate of U.S.

refineries is close to 95%, and there is limited room for further improvement. Citi predicts that strategic reserve replenishment will be difficult to sustainably absorb excess supply; China's inventory management has become more commercialized, and non-OECD economies are also subject to fiscal and storage capacity constraints. U.S.

liquid fuel production has increased by nearly 1 million barrels per day year-on-year as of April, and crude oil production has increased by approximately 350,000 barrels per day.

The report believes that if OPEC+ does not strengthen supply discipline, supply resilience, saturated refinery demand and weak Chinese demand will continue to put pressure on the oil market until 2027.

Goldman Sachs: AI investment will expand from computing infrastructure to the real economy, and capital demand may exceed US$7.6 trillion from 2026 to 2031 Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest report that AI is simultaneously promoting the expansion of digital infrastructure and the reconstruction of the real economy.

Manufacturing, energy, robotics, national defense and industrial software will become the core implementation scenarios in the next stage.

It is estimated that global AI capital expenditures around computing power, data centers and electricity will total approximately US$7.6 trillion from 2026 to 2031; of which annual investment will rise from US$765 billion in 2026 to US$1.64 trillion in 2031. Hyperscale cloud vendors may invest more than US$6 trillion in the AI field by 2030.

Goldman Sachs believes that the current impact of AI is first reflected in the software industry, but the opportunities are not limited to software. AI is expected to expand the addressable market for automation and enterprise software by approximately 2.5 times over the next decade.

However, the software industry is also facing a revaluation of valuations and business models: as of June this year, the iShares Expanded Technology Software ETF has fallen by about 17%, down about 26% from its October 2025 high.

The market value of the top ten constituent stocks has decreased by nearly US$800 billion, and the forward price-to-earnings ratio has compressed from about 35 times to 22 times.

The report points out that as basic models gradually become commoditized, pricing power will migrate to "result delivery, agent orchestration, proprietary data and business scenarios." Chinese models are rapidly expanding their share with lower inference costs.

OpenRouter data shows that their token consumption proportion has increased from low single digits at the end of 2024 to about half in early 2026, but the value will ultimately be concentrated in the data layer and result layer.

In the real economy, digital twins, industrial AI, predictive maintenance, logistics automation and robots are accelerating their implementation.

The Siemens Amberg factory has reduced downtime by 40% through AI predictive maintenance; the Figure robot has been running at the BMW factory for a total of about 1,250 hours and participated in the production of more than 30,000 cars.

Goldman Sachs predicts that the global humanoid robot market will grow from 20,000 units in 2025 to 1.4 million units in 2035, but large-scale commercialization is still subject to workflow integration, unit economics and financing structures.

Goldman Sachs emphasized that existing financing tools are still difficult to cover the high capital and long-term deployment needs of physical AI. Private credit, sovereign wealth funds, pension funds, ABS, CMBS and new joint venture structures will become key sources of funding.

Industrial AI software mergers and acquisitions and capital integration in robotics, national defense and energy infrastructure are expected to accelerate simultaneously.

Nomura: AI demand supports tight storage supply and demand, and new investment is difficult to change the boom pattern in the short term Nomura said in its latest research report that the market's concerns about the large-scale expansion of Korean memory chip manufacturers and Meta's planned sale of excess computing power may be amplified.

Neither of them is sufficient to indicate that the AI-driven storage boom cycle is weakening. The bank believes that the core contradiction in the current industry is still the tight supply caused by AI demand, rather than short-term overcapacity.

Recently, South Korean memory chip companies and related companies disclosed long-term investment plans with a total scale of approximately 4,800 trillion won, equivalent to approximately US$3.5 trillion, of which approximately 3,700 trillion won was directly invested in storage-related projects.

Relevant news has triggered market concerns about the rapid expansion of future supply of DRAM and NAND. However, Nomura pointed out that it usually takes many years for new projects to be announced, park construction and effective production capacity formed.

Taking the Yongren Semiconductor Industrial Park as an example, the project was launched about nine years ago and is expected to start small-scale production until the end of 2027 at the earliest. This means that the cycle from early investment to the actual release of influential supply may exceed ten years.

Therefore, the recently announced investment plans are unlikely to significantly change the supply and demand pattern of the industry in the next few years. Nomura believes that the storage market is still facing a structural shortage driven by AI demand.

Storage manufacturers are prioritizing the allocation of production capacity for high-bandwidth memory (HBM) with higher profit margins, squeezing the supply space of traditional DRAM and NAND.

As the construction of AI servers, inference applications and data centers continues to advance, the supply constraints of general storage products are likely to continue.

Regarding Meta’s plan to commercialize surplus data center computing power, Nomura believes that this is more like a regular move by large platforms to increase return on capital, rather than a signal of weakening demand for AI hardware.

The bank pointed out that the external sale of computing power can provide support for customers such as OpenAI and Anthropic, and expand the use of model training and inference by reducing computing costs.

Nomura predicts that the demand expansion brought about by the decline in computing power prices may continue to push up the utilization rate of AI infrastructure and maintain demand support for storage products such as HBM, DRAM and NAND.

A股2026年7月8日（星期三），市场日度分析报告

一、当日行情总览

7月8日，A股市场呈现“冲高回落、深V震荡”的格局，三大指数集体收跌。截至收盘，上证指数报3970.88点，跌0.49%，成交额11923.56亿元；深证成指报14939.73点，跌1.87%，成交额13711.60亿元；创业板指报3845.35点，跌1.70%，成交额6517.74亿元。

科创50成为主要宽基指数中唯一收红的指数，报2016.23点，涨0.73%。北证50表现最弱，跌2.59%。大盘股表现优于中小盘，市场风格偏向蓝筹。

沪深两市全天成交额约2.58万亿元，较前一交易日缩量约159亿元，已连续5个交易日缩量，创下4月8日以来的最低成交水平。全市场仅1593只个股上涨，3790只下跌，超3700只个股飘绿；56只涨停、44只跌停。市场情绪整体偏弱。

二、分时走势回顾

早盘：四大股指集体高开。开盘后指数一度快速下探，创业板指、深成指均一度跌超2%，但随后展开强势反弹。科创50率先发力，半日大涨3.13%，报2064.20点。截至午间收盘，沪指涨0.52%重返4000点，深成指涨0.08%，创业板指涨0.95%。半日成交额1.72万亿元，较上日放量736亿元。算力租赁、半导体芯片、油气等方向领涨。

午后：指数冲高后持续回落，三大股指由涨转跌。机器人、锂电池、有色金属等板块加速下挫，拖累主要股指转跌。最终三大指数集体收跌，科创50涨幅也由超3%收窄至0.73%，呈现典型的冲高回落走势。

三、板块与行业分析

今日市场结构性分化极为突出——计算机、煤炭、油气等板块逆势走强，新能源产业链全线重挫。

领涨板块

1. 计算机（+2.44%）：涨幅位居申万行业第一，主力资金净流入94.46亿元。网络安全、国资云、算力租赁等概念爆发涨停潮，深信服、网宿科技收获20CM涨停，浪潮信息、广电运通、云赛智联等多股涨停。消息面上，金融业网络安全管理升级，金融信创市场规模预计2026年突破3000亿元；SemiAnalysis报告强调算力需求远未达到峰值。

2. 油气开采及服务（+3.50%）：涨幅居全市场首位，通源石油涨幅超7%。受中东局势再度紧张刺激，国际原油期货价格上行，WTI原油上涨2.76%至70.44美元/桶，布伦特原油上涨3.01%至74.16美元/桶。

3. 煤炭（+1.20%）：晋控煤业涨幅超6%。中信证券预测样本煤炭上市公司二季度净利润环比增长大约31%。

4. 旅游及酒店（+1.64%）：岭南控股、西藏旅游涨停，利好来自国务院原则批复《旅游强国建设“十五五”规划》。

5. 贵金属：招金黄金拿下5天3板实现涨停，背景为央行连续20个月增持黄金。

领跌板块

1. 电力设备（-4.15%）：跌幅位居全市场首位，能源金属下跌6.55%，电池板块下跌5.32%。新能源产业链整体下行，固态电池、锂矿赛道领跌，盛新锂能等多只个股触及跌停。

2. 人形机器人：绿的谐波跌幅接近16%，前期板块累计涨幅偏高，迎来集中获利了结。

3. 创新药：赛伦生物跌幅超过13%，板块前期持续上行之后开启回调阶段。

四、个股与资金分析

特大单资金整体大幅净流出，两市当日特大单合计净流出253.49亿元。分行业来看，仅有9个行业获得特大单净流入，计算机行业净流入94.91亿元排名第一；余下22个行业呈现净流出，电子行业特大单净流出112.25亿元居于首位。

资金大幅流入个股

1. 紫光股份（+6.80%）：特大单净流入26.36亿元，单日净流入规模全市场第一

2. 浪潮信息（涨停）：特大单净流入22.10亿元

3. 网宿科技（20CM涨停）：特大单净流入15.23亿元

4. 中芯国际（+4.59%）：特大单净流入14.90亿元

5. 中际旭创（+0.57%）：特大单净流入14.51亿元

资金大幅流出个股

1. 长电科技（-6.81%）：特大单净流出17.57亿元，流出额度排名首位

2. 兆易创新（-2.71%）：主力净流出超过13亿元

3. 佰维存储（-6.77%）：特大单净流出13.49亿元

4. 华天科技（-1.64%）：主力净流出超过11亿元

五、重大消息面

① 成交额连续5日萎缩：当日2.58万亿的成交额创下4月8日以来新低，市场交投热情逐步降温。
② 央行逆回购操作：7月8日投放150亿元7天期逆回购，操作利率维持1.40%不变。
③ 央行连续20个月增持黄金：截至六月末国内黄金储备达到7544万盎司，相较5月新增48万盎司。
④ 国务院批复《旅游强国建设“十五五”规划》，目标搭建完善现代化旅游产业体系。
⑤ 金融监管总局划定风险底线，对冲大盘中期深度下行的可能性。

Daily Market Wrap 09/07/2026

Global markets remained focused on escalating tensions in the Middle East, particularly between the United States and Iran, which continued to drive volatility across financial markets. Energy prices surged, with both Brent and WTI crude oil rising more than 6% overnight after gaining over 5% in the previous session. 
The rally was fuelled by intensified military activity in the Gulf, increasing concerns about potential disruptions to global oil supplies and prompting investors to adopt a more defensive stance.

Fed meeting minutes show disagreement On July 8, local time, the minutes of the June Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting released by the Federal Reserve showed that the committee voted 12:0 and unanimously decided to maintain the target range of the federal funds rate at 3.5% to 3.75%.

However, the overall tone of the minutes was hawkish, which triggered the market's concern about the tightening of monetary policy. The minutes showed that participants generally believed that inflation would “further intensify” and be “well above” the long-term target of 2%.

Core PCE inflation rose to 3.3% in April, and staff estimate headline PCE inflation rose further to 4.1% in May. Participants attributed higher inflation to the ongoing impact of tariffs, conflicts in the Middle East driving up energy and input costs, and strong demand driven by artificial intelligence-related investments.

It is worth noting that a few participants clearly stated that there are "reasons to increase" the interest rate target range.

There were clear differences within the committee regarding the judgment on the policy interest rate at the end of the year: some participants believed that the interest rate should be maintained at the current level or slightly lower, while others believed that the interest rate should be higher than the current level.

In addition, the committee decided to remove language from its previous statement that suggested a possible future rate cut, sending a clear hawkish signal. However, the minutes also showed that the labor market remained stable and economic activity remained robust, supported by artificial intelligence investment and consumer spending.

Most participants believed that medium- and long-term inflation expectations are still anchored around 2%, but warned that if inflation remains high, it may begin to erode the stability of expectations.

Analysts believe that this minute shows that the Fed's policy balance between inflation and growth is tilting towards the anti-inflation side, and the market needs to be prepared for the possibility of "higher and longer" interest rates or even further interest rate increases.

Top Stories

The US Central Command stated that US forces have begun further strikes against Iranian targets. US President Trump declared the US-Iran memorandum of understanding “terminated,” and indicated potential restoration of a maritime blockade on Iran, though he clarified the blockade would “only target Iran.” Trump again threatened to strike key Iranian civilian infrastructure and suggested possibly taking control of Kharg Island, but later stated he did not believe another war with Iran was likely. He emphasized that vessel transit through the strait should be avoided until seafarer safety is guaranteed.

Selling pressure intensifies; South Korean equities enter technical bear market. On July 8, the Korea Composite Stock Price Index plunged again, triggering a circuit breaker and closing down 5.35% at 7,246.79, a nearly 23% retreat from its all-time high, entering technical bear market territory. South Korea’s financial regulator held an emergency meeting, publicly warning that excessive concentration in the semiconductor sector is amplifying market volatility. Leveraged ETFs contributing to market swings are drawing close scrutiny, with regulatory measures under consideration—including capping issuance volumes, imposing size limits, and raising retail investor entry thresholds.

The Fed’s June minutes revealed that while policymakers unanimously agreed to maintain rates steady, underlying policy divisions became apparent. Upside risks to inflation remain prominent; the minutes removed forward guidance on rate cuts and explicitly stated that further hikes remain possible if inflation stays elevated. At the same time, the prospect of rate cuts was retained, with emphasis that easing would be considered if inflation declines or economic conditions weaken. Future policy will be entirely data-dependent, with inflation, employment, and geopolitical developments serving as key variables determining the direction of monetary policy, ushering in a period of bidirectional caution.

China Macro & Policy

The National Science and Technology Awards Ceremony, the Chinese Academy of Sciences and Chinese Academy of Engineering Annual Conference, and the 11th National Congress of the China Association for Science and Technology were held in Beijing. General Secretary of the Communist Party of China Central Committee, President of the People’s Republic of China, and Chairman of the Central Military Commission Xi Jinping attended the event, presented awards to recipients of the National Top Science and Technology Award, and delivered a keynote speech. Xi emphasized that the “15th Five-Year Plan” period is a critical phase for building a science and technology powerhouse. It is essential to seize historical opportunities and meet contemporary challenges, accelerate high-level scientific and technological self-reliance and strength, steadily advance toward the goal of becoming a science and technology powerhouse by 2035, and use technological innovation to support and lead Chinese-style modernization.

The 2025 National Science and Technology Awards were announced, recognizing 258 projects and 11 scientists. The National Top Science and Technology Award was conferred upon Academician Chen Liquan and Academician Ben De. Chen Liquan is a pioneer and leader in China’s lithium battery field. Ben De is the founder of airborne pulse Doppler radar technology in China, a key innovator in phased array radar technology, and a forerunner in space-based surveillance radar technology.

The People’s Bank of China’s Monetary Policy Committee held its second-quarter meeting on July 4. The meeting reviewed the main policy direction for the coming period, recommending synergistic effects of incremental and existing policies, enhancing policy foresight, flexibility, and targeting, and carefully calibrating the strength, timing, and pace of policy implementation based on domestic and international economic and financial conditions. The meeting stressed continued support for financial services to private enterprises and maintaining stable financial market operations.

China’s Minister of Commerce Wang Wentao and the Netherlands’ Minister for Foreign Trade and Development Cooperation Schermer co-chaired the 18th China-Netherlands Economic and Trade Mixed Committee meeting, engaging in candid and in-depth exchanges on China-Netherlands and China-EU economic relations. Wang expressed hope that the Netherlands would create a fair, equitable, and predictable environment for Chinese enterprises investing in the country, maintain stability in semiconductor supply chains, and help resolve disputes involving specific companies.

Four ministries jointly issued the “Opinions on Accelerating the Application and Development of ‘AI + Human Resources and Social Security,’” outlining six key areas for developing major AI applications in HRSS, including digital employment and smart social insurance. The document also establishes a comprehensive map of 67 specific AI application scenarios within the HRSS sector.

EIA REPORT: FUNDAMENTALS REMAIN STABLE, BUT GEOPOLITICAL RISK IS DRIVING THE OIL MARKET

The latest EIA Weekly Petroleum Status Report paints a clear picture: oil market fundamentals remain relatively balanced, while geopolitical risk in the Middle East has become the dominant force behind price action.

Here are the key takeaways:

1. U.S. crude inventories increased

Commercial crude oil inventories (excluding the SPR) rose by 3.0 million barrels, from 408.4 million to 411.4 million barrels.

Under normal market conditions, this would be considered a bearish signal, indicating that physical crude supply remains sufficient.

⸻

2. U.S. crude production remains near record highs

Domestic production reached approximately 13.86 million barrels per day, continuing to hover near historical highs.

The EIA also noted that this week’s figure includes a benchmark adjustment, meaning the increase reflects statistical revisions rather than a sudden surge in actual production.

Overall, U.S. supply remains robust.

⸻

3. Refineries are running at very high utilization

U.S. refinery utilization climbed to 95.8%.

This suggests refiners continue to operate at an exceptionally strong pace, supported by steady fuel demand and healthy refining margins.

⸻

4. Gasoline and distillate inventories declined

During the week:

Gasoline inventories fell by 1.9 million barrels
Distillate (diesel) inventories dropped by 5.0 million barrels

Distillates are often viewed as a key indicator of industrial activity and freight demand, making this a supportive signal for petroleum consumption.

⸻

5. Net imports tightened

U.S. crude imports declined by roughly 654,000 barrels per day, while exports remained elevated.

This helps offset inventory builds and could limit supply pressure in the coming weeks.

⸻

Fundamentals alone would not justify a major rally

If this had been an ordinary week, the report would likely have placed mild downward pressure on oil prices.

But this is not an ordinary market.

Traders are largely looking past inventory data because geopolitical developments have become the primary driver.

Among the major factors currently influencing prices are:

The United States revoking waivers related to Iranian oil exports.
Rising military tensions between the U.S. and Iran.
Confirmed military operations by CENTCOM.
Attacks involving commercial shipping near the Strait of Hormuz.
Continued uncertainty surrounding Kharg Island, the hub for roughly 90% of Iran’s crude exports.

In today’s environment, geopolitical risk premium outweighs weekly inventory data.

⸻

What is the market pricing now?

The market is no longer focused solely on current supply.

Instead, it is pricing the probability of future disruptions, including:

A disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
A sharp reduction in Iranian oil exports.
Expanded U.S. military operations.
Iranian retaliation against regional assets.
Supply disruptions affecting Gulf producers.

As long as the perceived probability of these events increases, oil prices can continue rising—even if inventories remain comfortable.

⸻

What to watch next

The next major drivers for crude prices are likely to be:

• Military developments involving the U.S. and Iran.

• Any activity around Kharg Island.

• Commercial shipping flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

• OPEC+ policy responses.

• Additional restrictions on Iranian oil exports.

⸻

Bottom line

The latest EIA report suggests that the physical oil market is not experiencing a severe supply shortage. U.S. crude production remains strong, commercial inventories increased, and refinery operations continue at a high level.

However, fundamentals are currently taking a back seat to geopolitics. As long as tensions in the Middle East continue to escalate, oil prices are likely to react far more to military developments than to weekly inventory reports.

The oil market is not trading a blockade.

It is trading the probability that insurance, routing, and shipping behavior start changing before policymakers admit the risk has changed.

Signal: a Saudi-flagged crude tanker sustained damage while navigating the Strait of Hormuz, the latest incident in the chokepoint.

That matters because Hormuz risk transmits through logistics first, not spot barrels first. If shipowners, insurers, or charterers demand more compensation, the effective cost of moving crude rises even without a full supply disruption.

The market usually waits for production loss. The better signal is whether friction appears in cargo flows, freight, war-risk premiums, and refinery buying behavior.

Watch Brent, tanker rates, and any sign that Gulf exporters need to adjust loading or routing assumptions.

The headline is damage. The macro signal is a higher geopolitical tax on energy liquidity.
